# Security, Identity, and Governance

<span class="part-kicker">Part V</span>

This part explains the enterprise controls that turn a useful prototype into a system the organization can responsibly run. It covers Single Sign-On (SSO), OAuth2, OpenID Connect (OIDC), Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE), JSON Web Tokens (JWTs), tenant isolation, LLM security, AI risk management, and secure development.

## Flow Through This Part

<section class="flow-strip">
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Identity</span><p>Understand users, groups, tenants, claims, scopes, tokens, and validation.</p></article>
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Protect</span><p>Secure FastAPI routes and separate end-user identity from workload identity.</p></article>
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Isolate</span><p>Design tenant boundaries, row-level security, document access control, and tests.</p></article>
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Govern</span><p>Handle LLM threat modelling, risk registers, approvals, incidents, and release sign-off.</p></article>
</section>

## Industry Thread

In a notebook, access control is often your laptop login. In production, access control is a system design requirement. In an enterprise, you must show audit evidence that the assistant only used data the user was allowed to see.

## Running Case Study Link

The document Q&A assistant is not credible without identity propagation and document-level access control. Retrieval must filter by permissions before content enters the prompt.

## Visual Anchor

![OIDC and PKCE flow diagram showing browser, identity provider, token exchange, API validation, and scoped data access.](../assets/generated/security/oidc-pkce-flow.svg)

## Read Next

Read identity, OIDC/JWT validation, and multi-tenant access before the LLM security chapter. The strongest LLM safety posture still fails if basic authorization is wrong.
